section: Transverse tensile and fatigue properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys

context_update.summary: This chunk presents transverse tensile and fatigue properties of three Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys (A: Fe-29Mn-9.4Al-1.1C fully austenite; B: Fe-29Mn-9.1Al-0.6C with 10% ferrite; C: Fe-29Mn-9.2Al-0.26C with 55% ferrite) after solution treatment at 1050 °C for 1.5 h. Tensile tests (Table I) show that transverse specimens have slightly lower UTS and higher elongation than longitudinal ones, but differences are negligible. Fatigue S-N curves for alloys A and C show no orientation effect. For alloy B, longitudinal specimen BL has superior fatigue resistance over transverse BT; the mechanism involves slip-band initiation only in austenite, and crack propagation behavior controlled by ferrite string arrangement relative to stress direction. In BT, ferrite strings parallel to crack path allow cracks to cross austenite grains (Fig. 2), while in BL, perpendicular ferrite strings arrest cracks (Fig. 3). Key figures: Fig. 1 (S-N curves for BL/BT), Fig. 2 (crack propagation in BT), Fig. 3 (crack arrest in BL). Table I gives tensile data. The chunk establishes processing-structure-property links and explains anisotropy in alloy B via ferrite morphology.
key_variables: ferrite string arrangement (parallel or perpendicular to crack path)
microstructure: alloy A: fully austenite after solution treatment
slip bands only in austenite phase of alloy B
ferrite string morphology influences crack propagation
properties: Tensile properties in Table I: UTS, YS, elongation, RA, hardness for AL, AT, BL, BT, CL, CT
Fatigue: alloys A and C – no orientation effect; alloy B – BL > BT in fatigue life
S-N curve data for BL/BT in Fig. 1
mechanisms: Fatigue crack initiation from slip bands in austenite
No slip bands or cracks in ferrite phase
Ferrite strings parallel to crack: crack passes through austenite (BT)
Ferrite strings perpendicular to crack: ferrite arrests crack (BL)
figures_or_tables: Figure 1 (S-N curves of BL and BT)
Figure 2 (crack propagation across austenite grains in BT)
Figure 3 (crack impediment by ferrite in BL)

rolling_context_summary:
Summary: This chunk presents transverse tensile and fatigue properties of three Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys (A: Fe-29Mn-9.4Al-1.1C fully austenite; B: Fe-29Mn-9.1Al-0.6C with 10% ferrite; C: Fe-29Mn-9.2Al-0.26C with 55% ferrite) after solution treatment at 1050 °C for 1.5 h. Tensile tests (Table I) show that transverse specimens have slightly lower UTS and higher elongation than longitudinal ones, but differences are negligible. Fatigue S-N curves for alloys A and C show no orientation effect. For alloy B, longitudinal specimen BL has superior fatigue resistance over transverse BT; the mechanism involves slip-band initiation only in austenite, and crack propagation behavior controlled by ferrite string arrangement relative to stress direction. In BT, ferrite strings parallel to crack path allow cracks to cross austenite grains (Fig. 2), while in BL, perpendicular ferrite strings arrest cracks (Fig. 3). Key figures: Fig. 1 (S-N curves for BL/BT), Fig. 2 (crack propagation in BT), Fig. 3 (crack arrest in BL). Table I gives tensile data. The chunk establishes processing-structure-property links and explains anisotropy in alloy B via ferrite morphology.
Material systems: Fe-Mn-Al-C (Femnal) alloys; Fe-29Mn-9.4Al-1.1C (alloy A); Fe-29Mn-9.1Al-0.6C (alloy B); Fe-29Mn-9.2Al-0.26C (alloy C); austenite; ferrite
Sample aliases: AL (alloy A, longitudinal); AT (alloy A, transverse); BL (alloy B, longitudinal); BT (alloy B, transverse); CL (alloy C, longitudinal); CT (alloy C, transverse)
Processing route: hot forging; homogenization; cold rolling to 2 mm; solution treatment at 1050 °C for 1.5 h; oil quenching; transverse stress orientation for testing
Key variables: carbon content (1.1, 0.6, 0.26 wt%); phase fraction (fully austenite, 90/10 austenite/ferrite, 45/55 austenite/ferrite); stress orientation (longitudinal vs transverse); ferrite string arrangement (parallel or perpendicular to crack path)
Microstructure: alloy A: fully austenite after solution treatment; alloy B: 90% austenite + 10% ferrite; alloy C: 45% austenite + 55% ferrite; slip bands only in austenite phase of alloy B; ferrite string morphology influences crack propagation
Properties: Tensile properties in Table I: UTS, YS, elongation, RA, hardness for AL, AT, BL, BT, CL, CT; Fatigue: alloys A and C – no orientation effect; alloy B – BL > BT in fatigue life; S-N curve data for BL/BT in Fig. 1
Mechanisms: Fatigue crack initiation from slip bands in austenite; No slip bands or cracks in ferrite phase; Ferrite strings parallel to crack: crack passes through austenite (BT); Ferrite strings perpendicular to crack: ferrite arrests crack (BL)
Figures or tables: Figure 1 (S-N curves of BL and BT); Figure 2 (crack propagation across austenite grains in BT); Figure 3 (crack impediment by ferrite in BL); Table I (tensile properties)
